video_title: Britain Was Taken Over By The Anywheres | David Goodhart
gist: In a wide-ranging What Bitcoin Did interview, author David Goodhart argues AI could finally erode the status and job security of Britain's overproduced graduate class the way globalization hollowed out blue-collar manufacturing workers, and connects that shift to his 'anywhere vs somewhere' framework for explaining Brexit, populism, and the UK's unresolved 2029 election-realignment risk. Host Peter McCormack presses him on UK tax burdens, small-business regulation, and the money-supply/asset-inflation dynamic, while Goodhart lays out concrete policy proposals on land tax, social care wages, and pension-fund UK equity allocation.

themes.theme: AI and the Future of Graduate Labor

insights.claim: David Goodhart argues AI could do to Britain's 'graduate elite' what globalization did to factory workers, ending the automatic path to high-status, well-paid jobs for generalist university graduates who aren't in the top 5-10% of their field.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Signals a structural repricing of white-collar/graduate labor risk from AI, shifting the disruption narrative from the globalization-era blue-collar story to upper-white-collar workers.
insights.quote: This could be the end of the line for people with generalist academic skills.

insights.claim: Goodhart expects AI's disruption of white-collar work to unfold more slowly than commonly feared, drawing an analogy to historical fears that cars replacing horses would cause mass unemployment.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Tempers aggressive near-term AI-unemployment shock narratives that some market commentary is pricing in.
insights.quote: everybody feared that there that there'd be mass unemployment of all the young lads who looked after horses.

insights.claim: Goodhart singles out junior clerical, legal and accountancy work as already largely automated or semi-automated and set to shrink further under AI, and explicitly flags coding as a vulnerable career choice because AI can now do it.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Points to specific white-collar job categories facing near-term AI displacement risk, relevant to wage/employment forecasts in legal, accounting and junior software roles.
insights.quote: perhaps I should train as a plumber or an electrician or um not so much a coder obviously cuz cuz AI can do coding

insights.claim: Goodhart predicts skilled trades will see rising status and pay as AI erodes graduate-job premiums, citing the UK's national shortage of welders who can earn £60,000-£70,000 within three to four years of training.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A concrete wage data point supporting a thesis of rising trade compensation relative to entry-level graduate salaries, relevant to vocational-training and trade-labor investment themes.
insights.quote: we have we have a national shortage of welders. You know, within three or four years of training as a welder you can be earning 60 or 70 grand.

insights.claim: Goodhart suggests that as AI erodes the graduate class's automatic claim to high status and income, it could make that class more politically sympathetic to blue-collar workers already displaced by globalization, potentially softening the UK's 'anywhere vs somewhere' political divide.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A second-order political-economy implication: AI-driven status compression among elites could reduce rather than deepen UK political polarization, relevant to forecasting future policy coalitions.

themes.theme: UK Fiscal Drag and Tax Policy

insights.claim: Goodhart says UK earners between roughly £60,000-£70,000 and £150,000 face effective marginal tax rates of about 70-80% once lost personal allowances and child benefit tapering stack up, which he says is pushing skilled people to relocate to Dubai and the US.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A quantifiable UK tax-cliff problem that can be tracked as a leading indicator of high-earner emigration and talent flight from Britain.
insights.quote: if you're earning anything between sort of 60 70 and a kind of 150 grand, you you have all of these points where your marginal tax rate uh is sort of 70 or 80%.

insights.claim: Goodhart states the UK now has the most progressive tax system in Europe when comparing the tax burden on the top 10% of earners to the middle 10%, exceeding even the Scandinavian countries.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Challenges the 'UK under-taxes the rich' narrative sometimes used to justify further wealth-tax proposals, relevant context for UK fiscal/tax-policy debates.
insights.quote: we now have the most um progressive tax system in the whole of Europe.

insights.claim: Goodhart argues the UK's fiscal deterioration is mainly attributable to COVID-era furlough spending rather than lingering scarring from the 2008 financial crisis, which he says turned out to be comparatively small.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Reframes the source of UK structural deficit pressure toward pandemic-era stimulus design rather than the GFC, relevant to assessing UK gilt/fiscal-risk narratives.
insights.quote: The real scarring was was the was COVID and the furlough scheme.

insights.claim: Peter McCormack and David Goodhart agree that continual expansion of the money supply, such as COVID-era furlough financing, transfers value upward to existing asset owners rather than to wage earners.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: A Cantillon-effect framing directly relevant to hard-asset/bitcoin investors: inflationary financing benefits existing asset holders, a rationale often used for scarce-asset allocation.
insights.quote: it is a way it does extract value upwards rather than downwards.

insights.claim: Goodhart proposes a land value tax on unproductive wealth (using 'the Duke of Westminster' as his example) as an alternative revenue source that wouldn't penalize entrepreneurs, suggesting it could replace stamp duty and possibly council tax.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A specific tax-reform proposal that would reshape UK property/land taxation, relevant for real estate and land-holding investors watching for future UK tax-reform risk.
insights.quote: it's a way of trying to trying to get a few more billion out of the Duke of Westminster without without crushing the next sort of tech uni- unicorn creator.

insights.claim: Goodhart argues the UK's relatively low capital gains tax is a genuine economic asset worth protecting rather than raising.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Pushes back against current UK political pressure to raise capital gains tax, framing it as competitiveness-preserving policy risk to watch.
insights.quote: the fact that we have quite low capital gains tax is an is an asset.

themes.theme: UK Startup Ecosystem and Capital Allocation Gaps

insights.claim: Goodhart cites economist Andy Haldane's claim that the UK has more tech unicorns than any country outside the US and China, but says most get sold to US acquirers rather than scaling independently, due to smaller UK market size and a shallower pool of risk capital than in America.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Explains a persistent structural leakage in UK tech value creation where exit value accrues to US acquirers, relevant to UK tech-sector investment and IPO-pipeline analysis.
insights.quote: we have the highest number of tech unicorns, I think, anywhere outside China and America.

insights.claim: Goodhart states UK pension funds allocate only about 4% of assets to UK equities despite successive reform efforts by Jeremy Hunt and Rachel Reeves aimed at raising that share.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable data point on UK equity home-bias underinvestment, relevant to UK equity market liquidity/valuation debates and whether pension reform will actually move flows into UK-listed companies.
insights.quote: they invest like 4% in UK equities. I mean, it's ridiculous.

insights.claim: Goodhart highlights SearchLand, a UK PropTech startup founded by his son and university friends that scrapes local-authority planning data nationwide, as having become the largest company in its niche with strong venture backing despite the broader difficult UK business climate.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A named, concrete example of a scaling UK startup in the property-data/PropTech niche, useful as a specific data point for UK proptech deal-sourcing.
insights.quote: they're now I think the biggest the biggest company in the sector and they and they they got good people and they got they got a bunch of capital backing and they're doing very well.

insights.claim: Goodhart frames UK politics through an 'anywhere' (mobile, university-educated, roughly 25-30% of the population) versus 'somewhere' (rooted, place-attached, roughly 40-50% of the population) divide, arguing this cultural/education-based split, not the EU itself, was the real driver of the Brexit vote.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A durable framework for reading UK populist voting patterns as an elite-overrepresentation revolt rather than pure economic protest, useful for forecasting future UK election outcomes and policy swings.
insights.quote: the European Union was kind of collateral damage in that vote.

insights.claim: Goodhart argues the 2019 Boris Johnson-led Conservative 'realignment' that could have permanently reoriented UK politics toward 'somewhere' voters failed because of COVID, abandonment of the 'leveling up' agenda, and a post-Brexit 'quasi open door' immigration policy adopted to fill post-pandemic labor shortages.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Explains why post-Brexit UK politics remains unsettled rather than realigned, implying continued policy unpredictability risk through the current parliament.
insights.quote: they didn't have time to to to make Brexit work.

insights.claim: Goodhart's central 2029 UK election scenario is that only a Conservative-Reform coalition can reverse what he calls the 'overshoots' of recent decades (economic overregulation, high immigration, cultural marginalization).
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Lays out a specific electoral-coalition scenario for 2029 that would determine UK policy direction on tax, immigration and regulation, relevant for UK political-risk positioning.
insights.quote: the only way you do that is by electing a Tory reform coalition government. I mean, that's the only way I can see in 2029.

insights.claim: Goodhart warns that if Rupert Lowe's breakaway 'Restore' party (associated with figures he names as Harrison Pitt, Connor Tomlinson and Charlie Downs) splits 2-3% of the vote away from the Tories or Reform in key constituencies, it could let a Labour/Green/Lib Dem government back into power in 2029, which he calls a serious risk.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Names the specific new political entity and personnel to track as a wildcard that could fragment the UK populist-right vote ahead of 2029.
insights.quote: Restore taking 2 or 3% off either the Tories or Reform in different constituencies

insights.claim: Goodhart calls Kemi Badenoch's Conservative Party politically 'under-priced,' expecting economically frustrated but socially liberal higher-professional-class voters who won't back Reform to eventually swing to the Tories under her.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific voter-migration thesis relevant for gauging UK political sentiment shifts and Conservative Party recovery odds.
insights.quote: the Tories are kind of under-priced.

insights.claim: Goodhart names Danny Kruger (Reform UK) as the most impressive politician he's seen for his ability to discuss non-economic issues like family life and social cohesion, contrasting him with an otherwise heavily economics-focused political class.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Flags a specific rising political figure worth tracking within Reform UK's leadership bench.
insights.quote: Danny's Danny's the best he's got.

insights.claim: Goodhart is skeptical Andy Burnham can bridge the anywhere-somewhere divide, arguing Manchester itself functions as 'the London of the north' (about 25% students) and keeps pulling the brightest people away from struggling nearby towns like Rochdale, Bury and Bolton.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: Challenges the popular narrative of Burnham/Manchester as a model for UK regional revival, useful for assessing 'levelling up' and UK regional-investment narratives.
insights.quote: Manchester is essentially the kind of London of the north.

insights.claim: Goodhart predicts that if Andy Burnham becomes prime minister and fails to deliver visible change like Keir Starmer, voters who defected to Zack Polanski's Greens will likely return there rather than stay with Labour.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A specific voter-flow prediction relevant to modeling UK left-of-center vote fragmentation risk ahead of 2029.
insights.quote: those voters who went off to Zack Polanski come back to Burnham will probably go back to Zack Polanski.

insights.claim: Peter McCormack says UK small businesses like his coffee shop face such heavy combined business rates, energy costs and VAT that only a thin margin remains for profit, making it hard to run a business without being personally hands-on full-time.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: A first-hand, specific account of UK small-business cost-structure squeeze, relevant to UK SME/hospitality-sector margin and investment-risk assessment.
insights.quote: if you take a cup of coffee, it's like this small sliver at the top you might have a chance to make a profit.

insights.claim: McCormack argues UK employment rights and minimum-wage law have shifted work 'from a privilege to an entitlement,' which he links to difficulty finding and retaining reliable managers and staff for small businesses.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: A labor-market friction claim from an active UK employer, relevant to hiring-cost and staffing-availability assessments for UK SMEs.
insights.quote: we've turned work from a privilege to an entitlement through employment rights and minimum wage.

insights.claim: McCormack recounts UK expats in the US telling him lower US taxes leave people with more money to spend, creating a more 'bustling economy,' while the UK by contrast is 'designed as an economy to take' rather than one that moves.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack (host)
insights.so_what: An anecdotal but pointed comparative framing of US vs UK tax/growth dynamics shaping sentiment among UK entrepreneurs considering relocation.
insights.quote: I could I could not go back to the UK cuz it's designed as an economy to take.

themes.theme: Social Care as an Underinvested Public Service

insights.claim: Goodhart proposes creating a formal 'enhanced care practitioner' role with basic medical skills (taking vital signs, giving injections, drawing blood) to bridge social care and the NHS, arguing better preventative social care could reduce costly NHS hospital-bed occupancy.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A specific, actionable policy proposal for UK health/social-care workforce reform to track in future government announcements, relevant to healthcare-services investors.
insights.quote: can take uh can take vital signs, can give injections, can take blood, can do

insights.claim: Goodhart cites a data point that elderly falls cost the NHS roughly £4 billion a year, and argues social-care programs focused on balance-improving exercise could meaningfully cut that cost by reducing hospital admissions.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A quantified NHS cost-saving lever (preventative social care/exercise programs) relevant to healthcare-cost and social-care-investment analysis.
insights.quote: people falling over cost the NHS like sort of 4 billion pounds a year or something.

insights.claim: Goodhart argues the UK should raise pay for social-care workers by roughly £1-£1.50 an hour above the national minimum wage via a dedicated 'social care minimum wage' to address a shortage of care workers as more women who once filled those roles now pursue higher-education professional careers instead.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A specific, costed policy lever for addressing UK social-care staffing shortages, relevant for tracking future wage-policy changes affecting the care sector's cost base.
insights.quote: we just need to uh we need to have a a special social care minimum wage that's, you know, perhaps a pound, a pound 50 an hour higher than the than the national um minimum wage.

insights.claim: Goodhart says the UK's 'higher professional class' has grown from roughly 3-4% of the population in the 1960s-70s to about 12% today (35-40% including lower professionals), and this expansion has driven its Conservative vote share down from about 70% in 1974 to roughly 15% now.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A hard, quantified data point on the scale and voting-behavior shift of the UK's professional-managerial class, useful for modeling UK electoral demographics and policy implications on tax and regulation.
insights.quote: back in '74 election, 2/3, 70% of high professional people voted Tory. Now, it's probably 15% or something.

insights.claim: Goodhart confirms UK demographic projections point to the country no longer being majority-white by around 2060, framing this as a topic requiring open political discussion about downstream effects on voting blocs and social cohesion rather than being dismissed as inherently racist to raise.
insights.speaker: David Goodhart (guest, author & Policy Exchange researcher)
insights.so_what: A concrete demographic data point shaping UK immigration-policy debate and long-horizon political-risk modeling.
insights.quote: Oh, absolutely. 2060, yeah.
